title: Solar Loan vs Cash Purchase
description: Buying solar with cash offers the best ROI, but a solar loan allows you to go solar with less upfront cost.
winnerSummary: Cash purchase usually delivers the highest long-term savings because you avoid interest, but a solar loan can preserve cash and still provide ownership benefits.
bestForA: Homeowners with available cash who want maximum lifetime savings.
bestForB: Homeowners who want ownership without paying the full cost upfront.
costConsiderations: Compare total installed cost, interest, dealer fees, tax credit eligibility, monthly payment, and the opportunity cost of using cash. Ask lenders whether the quoted APR includes a dealer fee because two loans with the same payment can have different true costs.

faqs.question: Can I pay off a solar loan early?
faqs.answer: Many solar loans allow early payoff, but always check for prepayment penalties.

faqs.question: Is the lowest monthly payment the best solar loan?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. A low payment can come from a longer term or dealer fee. Compare total repayment, APR, term length, and prepayment rules.

faqs.question: Does paying cash change tax credit eligibility?
faqs.answer: Payment method alone does not usually determine federal tax credit eligibility, but ownership, qualifying costs, and tax liability matter. Verify with a tax professional.

sources: U.S. Department of Energy solar financing resources
Consumer Financial Protection Bureau financing education
EnergySage solar equipment and financing education
